coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor between nanoceria and graphene quantum dots for the determination of ochratoxin A
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Jiuying
Wei, Wenqi
Wang, Jiawen
Ji, Saijie
Chen, Guichan
Lu, Jusheng
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, colloidal cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were firstly synthesized by sol-gel method and pyrolysis respectively, which all have a uniform nano-size and significant fluorescence emission. Due to the fluorescence emission spectrum of nanoceria overlapped the absorption spectrum of GQDs, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nanoceria and GQDs could occur effectively by the electrostatic interaction. Based on it, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for the determination of ochratoxin A (OTA), a small molecular mycotoxin produced by Aspergillus and Penicillium strains, has been successfully constructed. In which, probe DNA1@nanoceria and DNA2@GQD were designed to complementary with OTA aptamer, both could adsorb each other, leading to the occur of FRET. After adding of OTA aptamer and then introducing of OTA, the FRET would be interrupted/recovered due to the specific affinity of OTA and its aptamer, the fluorescence recovery value would increase with the addition of OTA. Under the optimal experimental conditions (pH 7, mGQD/nanoceria 2, captamer 100 nM, incubation time 30 min), the constructed ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor exhibited a satisfying linear range (0.01–20 ng mL−1), low limit of detection (2.5 pg mL−1) and good selectivity towards OTA, and has been successfully applied for the analysis of real sample peanuts with good accuracy of the recoveries ranged from 90 to 110%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Ochratoxin A
Ratiometric FRET
GQD
Nanoceria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Coupling DNA1 to nanoceria (DNA1@nanoceria) and DNA2 to GQD (DNA2@GQD)
Construction of ratiometric FRET aptasensor for OTA determination
Preparation and characterization of nanoceria and GQD
Mechanism of FRET between nanoceria and GQDs
Construction of FRET aptasensor and optimization of experimental conditions
Specificity of the FRTE aptasensor and analytical application for OTA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions , the Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP) , the National Natural Science Foundations of China ( 21675067 ), the Undergraduate Students Project of Jiangsu Province ( 201610320001Z , 201610320122X ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Normal University ( 15XLR007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of nanoceria. Inset: HRTEM and size distribution of nanoceria. (B) TEM image of GQDs. Inset: HRTEM and size distribution of GQDs. (C) UV absorbance and fluorescence spectra (λex, 255 nm) of nanoceria. (D) UV absorbance and fluorescence spectra (λex, 360 nm) of GQDs. Inset: photograph of the GQD suspension under white light (left) and UV light (365 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution containing nanoceria (0.5 μg mL−1) and GQD with different concentrations (a, 0.0, b, 0.2, c, 0.4, d, 0.5, e, 0.6, f, 0.8, g, 1.0 μg mL−1). (B) Effect of GQD concentration on fluorescence intensity of nanoceria and GQD. λex, 255 nm.

Effect of Cr3+ with different concentrations (a, 0, b, 2, c, 5, d, 10, e, 15, f, 20 μg mL−1) on fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution containing nanoceria (0.5 μg mL−1) and GQD (1.0 μg mL−1). (B) Effect of NaOH or HCl solution (2.0 mM) added alternatively in the mixed solution containing nanoceria (0.5 μg mL−1), GQD (1.0 μg mL−1) and Cr3+ (20 μg mL−1) on fluorescence intensity of nanoceria and GQD. λex, 255 nm.
Schematic process of effect of metal cation on FRET between nanoceria and GQD under different condition.
Schematic process of FRET aptasensor for OTA determination.
Comparison of the proposed method for OTA biosensing with other previously reported works.
Determination of OTA with the proposed FRET aptasensor (mean ± SD, n = 6).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor between nanoceria and graphene quantum dots for the determination of ochratoxin A

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Green Synthetic Chemistry for Functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, Xuzhou 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Green Synthetic Chemistry for Functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jiangsu Normal University, 101 Shanghai Road, Xuzhou 221116, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, colloidal cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were firstly synthesized by sol-gel method and pyrolysis respectively, which all have a uniform nano-size and significant fluorescence emission. Due to the fluorescence emission spectrum of nanoceria overlapped the absorption spectrum of GQDs, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between nanoceria and GQDs could occur effectively by the electrostatic interaction. Based on it, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor for the determination of ochratoxin A (OTA), a small molecular mycotoxin produced by Aspergillus and Penicillium strains, has been successfully constructed. In which, probe DNA1@nanoceria and DNA2@GQD were designed to complementary with OTA aptamer, both could adsorb each other, leading to the occur of FRET. After adding of OTA aptamer and then introducing of OTA, the FRET would be interrupted/recovered due to the specific affinity of OTA and its aptamer, the fluorescence recovery value would increase with the addition of OTA. Under the optimal experimental conditions (pH 7, mGQD/nanoceria 2, captamer 100 nM, incubation time 30 min), the constructed ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor exhibited a satisfying linear range (0.01–20 ng mL−1), low limit of detection (2.5 pg mL−1) and good selectivity towards OTA, and has been successfully applied for the analysis of real sample peanuts with good accuracy of the recoveries ranged from 90 to 110%.

ce:section.ce:para: Ochratoxin A (OTA) is a mycotoxin as a secondary metabolite derived from various Aspergillus and Penicillium strains, which is known to cause nephrotoxic, immunotoxic, teratogenic and carcinogenic effects in humans and animals [1,2]. OTA is chemically stable and widely occurs in feed and food chains due to the contamination of foodstuffs prior to harvest or during storage [3], and European Commission has established a maximum acceptable OTA level of 5 μg kg−1 for raw cereal grains [4]. Therefore, it is of considerable significance to develop a rapid and sensitive assay for OTA detection to ensure food safety and avoid the risk of OTA consumption. At present, several analytical methods including high performance liquid chromatography [5,6], electrochemistry [7,8], electrochemiluminescence [9], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and aptamer-based biosensing [10–13], have been reported for OTA determination.

Different from antibodies, aptamers are DNA or RNA molecules which can adopt specific three-dimensional conformations to bind target analytes with a high affinity and specificity, which are characterized by high selectivity, high stability, low cost and high thermal stability under a broad array of conditions [14,15]. Aptamers for OTA were selected in 2008 and exhibited high specificity to OTA [16,17], which binding affinities, determined by equilibrium dialysis, were in the nanomolar range [16]. Therefore, many aptasensors combined with fluorescence [11,12,18–20], electrochemical [21,22], surface plasmon resonance [23] and differential pulse voltammetry [24] have been constructed for detecting OTA, and these methods open a new avenue for the detection of OTA.

As a better technique than simple fluorescence, FRET has been widely applied as a sensing mode [25–28], which is a nonradiative phenomenon with the energy transferred from an excited donor fluorophore to a proximal ground-state acceptor through intermolecular dipole-dipole coupling. Generally, the FRET probes have been developed based on the fluorescent quenching or enhancement mechanism. However, such single-wavelength based measurement signal would be easily interfered by various external factors, limiting the accuracy and sensitivity of quantitative determination. Ratiometric FRET strategy, which involves the simultaneous measurement of two fluorescence signals at different wavelengths and use the ratio of the two fluorescence intensities to quantitatively detect the analytes, can alleviate most of the interferences and give greater precision [29–32].

Excellent donor-acceptor pair is a significant factor desired to improve the efficiency of ratiometric FRET and the analytical performance. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon fluorescent nanomaterials [33–36], have superior properties including stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, and high water solubility compared with traditional semi-conductor quantum dots containing toxic heavy-metals [37–39], which could be designed as a good fluorescent probe for biosensing. On the other hand, due to the diffusion and reactivity of oxygen vacancies, cerium dioxide nanoparticle (nanoceria) is a unique and promising nanomaterial, in which, the formed oxygen vacancies are associated with a conversion of cerium ions from Ce4+ to Ce3+ [40]. Under near-UV excitation, excited Ce3+ ions to the ground state can emit fluorescence signal, which has successfully applied for a fluorescence probe [41,42].

In the present work, colloidal nanoceria and GQDs were firstly synthesized by sol-gel method and pyrolysis respectively, which all showed excellent fluorescence properties. Based on the mechanism of FRET between nanoceria and GQDs, a sensitive and selective aptasensor for the determination of OTA was constructed. In which, probe DNA1 and DNA2 oligonucleotides were selected to complementary with OTA aptamer, and DNA1@nanoceria and DNA2@GQD were synthesized by chemical bonding. When DNA1@nanoceria mixed with DNA2@GQD, the FRET would occur due to the electrostatic interaction between nanoceria and GQD. Addition of OTA aptamer would limit the FRET because of a large distance between nanoceria and GQD. Furthermore, after introduction of target OTA, FRET from nanoceria to GQD would recover again due to the specific affinity of OTA and its aptamer, which could be applied for quantitative analysis of OTA in real sample.

Ochratoxin A (OTA), citric acid, N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethyl-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). OTA aptamer (DNA) was selected according to the literature [16], and its sequence is 5′-GAT CGG GTG TGG GTG GCG TAA AGG GAG CAT CGG ACA-3’. Probe DNA1 and DNA2 oligonucleotides were complementary to OTA aptamer and their sequences were 3′-NH2-(TTT)3 CTA GCC CAC ACC-5'and 3′-CTC GTA GCC TGT (TTT)3-NH2-5′, respectively. All of DNAs were synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise noted, all chemicals and materials were used as received. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system and used in all experiments.

The SEM images were recorded with a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan). The TEM and HRTEM images were performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). UV-vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-4600 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The nanoceria was synthesized by sol-gel method and described as follows. Under vigorous stirring, hydrogen peroxide solution (30 vol%) was slowly added into 20 mL of 0.1 M Ce(NO3)3 aqueous solution dropwise, making the solution from initially colorless to brown and eventually orange. Then concentrated ammonia solution was added rapidly to the above stirred solution, and a lot of blood red precipitate would appear immediately, boiled and cooled to room temperature (RT). After separation, the precipitate was dispersed in water and adjusted the pH to 1–1.5, then heated to reflux until a yellow-green nanoceria hydrosol was generated. Then, 2 mL of polyvinyl alcohol solution (PVA, 10 m/v%) and 2 mL of urea solution (5 m/v%) were added in to the above nanoceria hydrosol, boiled for 40 min, centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol, then dried at 80–85 °C overnight. So, a weakly-agglomerated nanoceria powder was obtained.

GQDs solution was prepared from the pyrolyzed citric acid (CA) according to the reported literature with some modification [43]. Briefly, in a round-bottomed flask, 2 g CA was heated at 200 °C for 30 min, and the colorless CA solids turn gradually into orange liquid to gain GQDs. Then, the orange liquid was adjusted to be 7.0 by 10 g L−1 NaOH solution under stirring. The obtained GQDs solution was used as stock solution and stored at 4 °C when not used, the concentration was about 2.0 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling DNA1 to nanoceria (DNA1@nanoceria) and DNA2 to GQD (DNA2@GQD)
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of DNA1@nanoceria, 0.5 mL of 1.0 μM nanoceria anhydrous ethanol dispersion was added in 2.0 mL of dried dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) containing 1.0 mg carbonyl diimidazole at 4 °C for 12 h in the dark, and then centrifuged. The activated nanoceria was added in a 2.0 mL of 10 mM pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution containing 1.0 μM DNA1 for 12 h. After centrifugation and washing with water, the complexes of DNA1 and nanoceria (DNA1@nanoceria) was dispersed in 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.0) to final volume of 5.0 mL and stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

In the process of preparation of DNA2@GQD, 0.5 mL of the as-prepared GQDs' suspension was mixed with 0.2 mL of 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing 4 mg mL−1 EDC and 2 mg mL−1 NHS for 0.5 h to obtain the activated GQDs. Followed by 2.0 mL of DNA2 solution (1.0 μM in 10 mM pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution) was added into the above solution and left at 4 °C overnight in the dark. The resultant DNA2@GQD were collected by centrifugation and washed with water for several times. Then they were dispersed in 10 mM pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution to final volume of 5.0 mL and stored at 4 °C for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric FRET aptasensor for OTA determination
ce:section.ce:para:
50 μL of DNA2@GQD solution was added in 0.5 mL of DNA1@nanoceria solution and mixed, then 50 μL of OTA aptamer solution (1.0 μM in 10 mM pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution) was added in the above solution and incubated for 30 min at RT. Subsequently, different amounts of OTA were added and mixed thoroughly. 30 min later, the emission fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 300–500 nm at an excitation wavelength of 255 nm. The concentration of OTA could be determined by the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm–360 nm (I
450
/I
360 ).

The peanut samples were ground by a high-speed disintegrator and passed through a 100-mesh sieve. And 5.0 g of ground peanut sample was added to 10 mL methanol aqueous solution (80 vol%) and vortexed for 5 min for sufficient extraction at RT. After centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min, 1.0 mL of the supernatant was mixed with 4.0 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) and then Tween 20 was added to a final volume of 10 mL. 50 μL of the mixed solution was used for the FRET measurement to determine the concentration of OTA. At the same time, a recovery test was carried out in accordance with the same procedure to demonstrate the validity of the proposed aptasensor. Blank sample was spiked with different amount of OTA standard solutions to produce a series of spiked solutions (OTA concentration, 0.01, 0.20, 1.00, 5.00 and 20.0 ng mL−1) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of nanoceria and GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the colloidal nanoceria was firstly prepared by sol-gel method, in which, Ce(III) nitrate was selected as a precursor, hydrogen peroxide was an oxidant for oxidation Ce(III) to Ce(IV), and ammonia was used as a catalyst. The TEM image showed that (Fig. 1 A), the as-prepared nanoceria particles have a uniform size in the range of 2.0–5.0 nm with an average size of ∼ 3.0 nm (inset of Fig. 1A), the HRTEM image of nanoceria (inset of Fig. 1A) illustrated the interplanar spacings of 0.31 nm and 0.27 nm respectively corresponding to the (111) and (100) crystal facets of the ceria crystal structure [44]. Moreover, the prepared nanoceria could be homogeneously dispersed in an aqueous solution, such suspension showed a good colloidal stability, which could be stable for 30 days and supported by the well-defined Tyndall effect (inset in Fig. S1). Meanwhile, in the medium solution from pH 2.0 to 8.0, the zeta potential of nanoceria (Malven zetasizer nano ZS, England) was relatively unchanged in the range of 28–30 mV (curve a of Fig. S1), showing that nanoceria was positively charged, and could be stably dispersed in an aqueous solution [45]. Furthermore, as shown in curve a of Fig. 1C, nanoceria had a typical UV absorption peak at 232 nm, and had a fluorescence intensity at the peak of 360 nm under the excitation of 255 nm (curve b of Fig. 1C).

GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) at 200 °C for about 30 min. The prepared GQDs were negatively charged in the aqueous solution from pH 6.0 to 12.0 (curve b of Fig. S1), and were fairly uniform and well dispersed, which size distribution was ca. 4.5–11.0 nm, with an average size of 8.0 nm (Fig. 1B). The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1B) indicated that GQDs have a high crystalline structure, with a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm, which is corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringes of grapheme [46]. Furthermore, GQDs had two absorption peaks at 235 and 339 nm in UV-vis absorption spectrum (curve a of Fig. 1D), which correspond to the π-π* transition of C=C and n-π* transition of C=O, respectively [47,48]. In addition, GQDs could emit an intensely blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light with 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1D). The fluorescence emission wavelength of GQDs at 450 nm was nearly excitation-independent with the excitation wavelength in the range of 300 nm and 400 nm (Fig. S2A), and the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm (curve b of Fig. 1D). However, when the excitation wavelength was below 280 nm, GQDs had almost no fluorescence emission (Fig. S2A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of FRET between nanoceria and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: When different amount of GQDs was added to 0.5 mL of pH 7.0 Tris-HCl buffer solution containing 0.5 μg mL−1 nanoceria, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were shown in Fig. 2 A. At the excitation wavelength of 255 nm, the fluorescence intensity at 360 nm decreased gradually with the addition of GQDs, meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm increased gradually. When the amount of GQDs added was 1.0 μg mL−1 or more, fluorescence intensity at 360 nm or fluorescence intensity at 450 nm reached to a constant value (Fig. 2B), which was different from that of Fig. S2B, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm increased with the increase of GQDs amount at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. It was because that, an effective FRET process could only occur when the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor overlap appreciably and the mutual distance of the donor-acceptor pair is smaller than 10 nm [49]. In the present work, nanoceria remained positively charged and GQDs was negatively charged in pH 7.0 solution, GQD could combine with nanoceria by electrostatic interaction, leading to a short distance between nanoceria and GQD. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectrum of nanoceria overlapped the absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. S3). Therefore, an effective FRET process could occur from nanoceria to GQDs, the energy-transfer efficiency could reach to 95%.

For confirming the FRET between GQD and nanoceria due to the electrostatic interaction, we firstly added different anions, such as PO4 3−, SO4 2−, NO3 − or Cl− (10 μg mL−1) into the mixed solution containing nanoceria (0.5 μg mL−1) and GQD (1.0 μg mL−1). The results showed that (Fig. S4), the addition of PO4 3− ion led to the recovery of the fluorescence emission at 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 360 nm only partially recovered when adding SO4 2− ion. However, the fluorescence intensity almost remained unchanged at 360 nm or 450 nm after addition of NO3 − or Cl− ion. It may be because that, nanoceria was positively charged, and would combine with the anions added by electrostatic interaction, preventing the direct contact between GQD and nanoceria, leading to the weakening of FRET. The greater the charge of the anions, the stronger the interaction between nanoceria and the anions such as PO4 3−, SO4 2−, the FRET would be weakened obviously (Fig. S4 A and B).

To further verify the mechanism of FRET between GQD and nanoceria, different cations were also added into the mixed solution of GQD and nanoceria. The cations with high valence, such as Cr3+, Fe3+, were more likely to combine with GQD which was negatively charged, leading to ceria remove from GQD, the FRET would be significantly affected (Fig. S5). In addition, the FRET decreased with the increase of Cr3+ concentration (Fig. 3 A). When the added Cr3+ concentration was 20 μg mL−1, the FRET had been completely interrupted. Furthermore, when the mixed solution containing nanoceria, GQD and Cr3+ was treated by NaOH solution (2.0 mM), the FRET between nanoceria and GQD would be recovered. It was because that, treated by NaOH, Cr3+ in the solution would be converted to neutral Cr(OH)3, nanoceria re-combined with GQD, leading to the recovery of FRET. If 2.0 mM HCl solution was added to the above mixed solution, the neutral Cr(OH)3 would be converted to Cr3+ again, the FRET would be interrupted again (Fig. 3B). The recovery/interruption cycle of FRET could continue by the addition of NaOH and HCl solution alternatively, and the schematic process was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Therefore, all of these indicated the FRET between nanoceria and GQD happened due to the strong electrostatic interaction each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of FRET aptasensor and optimization of experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the mechanism of FRET between GQD and nanoceria, a fluorescence aptasensor for the determination of OTA was constructed and the schematic process was illustrated in Scheme 2 . When DNA2@GQD solution was added in DNA1@nanoceria solution and mixed, FRET would happen due to the electrostatic interaction, leading to the increase of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm and the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 360 nm (curve a in Fig. S6). After adding OTA aptamer in the above solution, the aptamer was hybridized with DNA1 and DNA2 to form a rod-like double helix. GQD and nanoceria were at both ends of the double-stranded DNAs, a large distance between each other would limit effective energy transfer from nanoceria to GQD, which led to the recovery of fluorescence intensity at 360 nm and the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (curve b in Fig. S6). The results showed that, when the concentration of OTA aptamer was 100 nM and the incubation time was 30 min or more, the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm and 360 nm (I
450
/I
360 ) reached the minimum and tend to a stable value (Fig. S7A and curve a of Fig. S7B), too high concentration of OTA aptamer would affect the sensitivity of the aptasensor. Furthermore, when target OTA was introduced into the system, due to the specific affinity, the aptamer combined with OTA, and nanoceria re-combined with GQD by electrostatic interaction, resulting in the recovery of FRET and the increase of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm (curve c in Fig. S6). After incubation for 30 min, the value of I
450
/I
360 increased and reached to the stable (curve b of Fig. S7B). Under the optimal experimental conditions, the increase of I450/I360 was dependent on the OTA concentration, which was linear proportional to the concentration of OTA in the ranges of 10.0 pg mL−1 to 20 ng mL−1, the linear regression equation was I
450
/I
360 = 0.453ρ (ng mL −1
)-0.0116 (R2 = 0.9957). And a limit of detection (LOD) of such aptasensor based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was 2.5 pg mL−1 (Fig. 4 ). Compared to other assays summarized in Table 1 , the proposed FRET method for OTA determination exhibited a satisfying linear range and limit of detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the FRTE aptasensor and analytical application for OTA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the FRET aptasensor for OTA, other structural analogs (Ochratoxin B, OTB; Ochratoxin C, OTC) or other mycotoxins (Aflatoxin B1, AFB1; Zearalenone, ZEN) was selected to replace OTA. As shown in Fig. S8, the presence of 1 mg L−1 AFB1 or ZEN had a negligible impact on I450/I360 value, whereas the presence of 1 mg L−1 OTB or OTC resulted in very slightly increased I450/I360 value. It was because that, OTB and OTC were structural analogs of OTA, which to some extent, still possessed a very weak combination ability with OTA aptamer. However, AFB1 and ZEN were distinct from OTA; thus, fluorescence responses were almost negligible. The above results indicated that the FRET aptasensor for OTA detection had outstanding specificity against structural analogs and other mycotoxins. In addition, in the work, the potential coexisting ions in the real sample, such as Fe3+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, may affect the determination of OTA, which could be removed by a R-H cation exchange resin and a R-OH anion exchange resin, respectively.

For evaluating analytical reliability and application potential of the proposed FRET aptasensor, the concentration of OTA in real peanut sample was determined and the spiked experiment was carried out. As seen in Table 2 , when the added concentration of OTA was 0.1, 0.2, 1.0, 5.0 and 20.0 ng mL−1, the relative standard deviation values of the analytical results were less than 11%, and the recoveries for the spiked samples ranged from 90% to 110%. In addition, when OTA standard solutions with different concentration were added into the real sample solution respectively, the plotted work curve was almost same as the calibration curve (Fig. S9). All of these implied the FRET aptasensor had a good accuracy in a wide linear range and was fully applicable to the real sample analysis.

In the present work, the FRET between synthesized nanoceria and GQDs could occur effectively by the electrostatic interaction, and could be interrupted/recovered controllably by the adding of OTA aptamer and target OTA. Based on this mechanism, a ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for OTA was constructed based on FRET between nanoceria and GQD in which, DNA1@nanoceria and DNA2@GQD were synthesized and mixed, leading to the occur of FRET due to the electrostatic interaction. After adding OTA aptamer and OTA, FRET from nanoceria to GQD would be interrupted and recovered due to the specific affinity of OTA and its aptamer, and the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm and 360 nm would increase with the increase of OTA concentration. Compared with other aptasensors reported and single-wavelength-based fluorescence measurement, the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for OTA based on FRET is simple to operate and has a low background signal, which exhibits a satisfying linear range (0.01–20 ng mL−1), low limit of detection (2.5 pg mL−1) and good selectivity, and could offer a promising feature for the analytical application in complex real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, the Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (21675067), the Undergraduate Students Project of Jiangsu Province (201610320001Z, 201610320122X), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Normal University (15XLR007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supporting information Supporting information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.08.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ochratoxin A: an overview on toxicity and carcinogenicity in animals and humans
Commission regulation (EC) no 1881/2006 of 19 December 2006 setting maximum levels for certain contaminants in foodstuffs
A reliable liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry method for simultaneous determination of multiple mycotoxins in fresh fish and dried sea foods
Determination of ochratoxin A in Italian red wines by molecularly imprinted solid phase extraction and HPLC analysis
An electrochemical immunosensor for ochratoxin A determination in wines based on a monoclonal antibody and paramagnetic microbeads
Horseradish peroxidase-screen printed biosensors for determination of ochratoxin A
Electrochemiluminescence biosensor for ultrasensitive determination of ochratoxin A in corn samples based on aptamer and hyperbranched rolling circle amplification
PVP-coated graphene oxide for selective determination of ochratoxin A via quenching fluorescence of free aptamer
Multiplexed fluorescence resonance energy transfer aptasensor between upconversion nanoparticles and graphene oxide for the simultaneous determination of mycotoxins
A signal-on fluorescent aptasensor based on Tb3+ and structure-switching aptamer for label-free detection of ochratoxin A in wheat
Aptamer-based single-walled carbon nanohorn sensors for ochratoxin A detection
Selection and characterization of single stranded DNA aptamers recognizing fumonisin B-1
Aptamer-based luminescence energy transfer from near-infrared-to-near-infrared upconverting nanoparticles to gold nanorods and its application for the detection of thrombin
Determination of ochratoxin A with a DNA aptamer
Fluorescence polarization based displacement assay for the determination of small molecules with aptamers
